perturbation.original_text: 1.15 "Professional Component" shall mean the performance of the professional component of the steps of the Assay, which is the interpretation of results (generated in the Technical Component) of an Assay by a pathologist, and is covered by CPT codes from the Professional Fee Schedule with the modifier "26".
perturbation.changed_text: 1.15 "Professional Component" shall mean the optional performance of some steps of the Assay, which is sometimes the interpretation of results (generated in the Technical Component) of an Assay by a pathologist, and is covered by CPT codes from the Professional Fee Schedule with or without the modifier "26".
perturbation.explanation: By making the Professional Component "optional" and removing the specification for the '26' modifier, the definition now contradicts established billing practices and standards for medical procedures. This change introduces uncertainty about what constitutes the Professional Component and how it should be billed, potentially leading to non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation details how physician services are to be paid. By removing the specification for modifier 26, we are conflicting with the defined procedure for billing professional services of the assay, creating a divergence from mandated Medicare/Medicaid billing processes.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 CFR § 414.1 - Basis and scope. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 42 CFR § 414.1 - Basis and scope. CFR prev | next § 414.1 Basis and scope . This part implements the following provisions of the Act: 1802—Rules for private contracts by Medicare beneficiaries. 1833—Rules for payment for most Part B services. 1834(a) and (h)—Amounts and frequency of payments for durable medical equipment and for prosthetic devices and orthotics and prosthetics. 1834(l)—Establishment of a fee schedule for ambulance services. 1834(m)—Rules for Medicare reimbursement for telehealth services. 1834A—Improving policies for clinical diagnostic laboratory tests 1842(o)—Rules for payment of certain drugs and biologicals. 1847(a) and (b)—Competitive bidding for certain durable medical equipment , prosthetics, orthotics, and supplies (DMEPOS). 1848—Fee schedule for physician services . 1881(b)—Rules for payment for services to ESRD beneficiaries. 1887—Payment of charges for physician services to patients in providers. [ 67 FR 9132 , Feb. 27, 2002, as amended at 69 FR 1116 , Jan. 7, 2004; 71 FR 48409 , Aug. 18, 2006; 81 FR 41098 , June 23, 2016] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract creates a contradiction by making the 'Professional Component' optional and removing the '26' modifier. The Law Explanation states this conflicts with the defined procedure for billing professional services and diverges from mandated Medicare/Medicaid billing processes. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 'fee schedule for physician services', implying a regulated billing process. By making the component optional and removing the modifier, the changed text undermines this regulated process.

perturbation.original_text:
5.  REGULATORY COMPLIANCE
5.1 Compliance with Laws. Biocept and Life Technologies and their respective Affiliates each agree to perform their respective obligations under this Agreement in compliance with all applicable Laws, in the Territory, including but not limited to applicable regulations, rules, and policies of third party payers that pay for the Assay.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.  REGULATORY COMPLIANCE
5.1 Compliance with Select Laws. Biocept and Life Technologies and their respective Affiliates each agree to perform their respective obligations under this Agreement in compliance with a selection of applicable Laws, in the Territory, only including specifically mentioned regulations, rules, and policies of third party payers that pay for the Assay.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting compliance to a "selection" of laws instead of "all applicable Laws", the contract language now conflicts with standard legal expectations and requirements for businesses to adhere to all relevant regulations. This creates a loophole where the parties could argue they are only obligated to comply with a subset of laws, potentially violating other unmentioned, but relevant laws. This modification creates legal uncertainty and potential non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The False Claims Act imposes liability on persons and companies who defraud governmental programs. By limiting the scope of laws that must be followed, this can be interpreted to allow for false claims to be submitted to the government under the pretense that certain regulations were not applicable under the contract.

False claims (a) Liability for Certain Acts.- (1) In general .-Subject to paragraph (2), any person who- (A) knowingly presents, or causes to be presented, a false or fraudulent claim for payment or approval; (B) knowingly makes, uses, or causes to be made or used, a false record or statement material to a false or fraudulent claim; (C) conspires to commit a violation of
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Civil Division | The False Claims Act Skip to main content MENU Fraud Section Practice Areas The False Claims Act Press Releases Fraud Recovery Statistics (PDF) Report Fraud Contact the Fraud Section The False Claims Act Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email Many of the Fraud Section’s cases are suits filed under the False Claims Act (FCA), 31 U.S.C. §§ 3729 - 3733, a federal statute originally enacted in 1863 in response to defense contractor fraud during the American Civil War. The FCA provides that any person who knowingly submits, or causes to submit, false claims to the government is liable for three times the government’s damages plus a penalty that is linked to inflation . FCA liability can arise in other situations, such as when someone knowingly uses a false record material to a false claim or improperly avoids an obligation to pay the government. Conspiring to commit any of these acts also is a violation of the FCA. In addition to allowing the United States to pursue perpetrators of fraud on its own, the FCA allows private citizens to file suits on behalf of the government (called “ qui tam ” suits) against those who have defrauded the government. Private citizens who successfully bring qui tam actions may receive a portion of the government’s recovery. Many Fraud Section investigations and lawsuits arise from such qui tam actions. The Department of Justice obtained more than $2.9 billion in settlements and judgments from civil cases involving fraud and false claims against the government in the fiscal year ending Sept. 30, 2024. More information about those recoveries can be found here and the 2024 FCA statistics can be found here .” Updated January 15, 2025
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by limiting compliance to a "selection" of applicable laws, directly contradicts the principles of the False Claims Act (FCA). The FCA, as outlined in both scraped snippets, imposes liability on those who knowingly submit false claims to the government. By stating that only a selection of laws will be followed, the contract creates a loophole that could allow parties to argue that they are not obligated to comply with all relevant regulations, thus potentially enabling false claims under the guise of non-applicable regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the FCA applies when someone knowingly uses a false record material to a false claim, implying the necessity of adherence to all relevant laws to prevent such actions. Therefore, the modification undermines the FCA's intent and creates a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text:
10. CONFIDENTIALITY
10.1 Confidential Information. Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing by the parties, each party agrees that, during the Term and for five (5) years thereafter, such party (the "Receiving Party") shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose and shall not use for any purpose, other than as expressly provided for in this Agreement, any information furnished to it by or on behalf of the other party (the "Disclosing Party") pursuant to this Agreement (collectively, "Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text:
10. CONFIDENTIALITY
10.1 Confidential Information. Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing by the parties, each party agrees that, during the Term and only for one (1) year thereafter, such party (the "Receiving Party") shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose and shall not use for any purpose, other than as expressly provided for in this Agreement, any information furnished to it by or on behalf of the other party (the "Disclosing Party") pursuant to this Agreement (collectively, "Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from five years to one year weakens the protection of confidential information. Certain trade secrets or intellectual property may require a longer protection period to maintain their value, and a one-year limit might be insufficient and misaligned with standard practices. It potentially contradicts the reasonable measures required to protect trade secrets, especially in the biotechnology/pharmaceutical area, where product life cycles can be far longer than 1 year.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: This act protects trade secrets. By reducing the confidentiality period to one year, the agreement may fail to provide reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially leading to their misappropriation and loss of legal protection under CUTSA. The limited timeframe undermines the ongoing efforts to maintain the secrecy of valuable information.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, which creates a potential contradiction with the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), as it may not provide 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that reducing the confidentiality period 'may fail to provide reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially leading to their misappropriation and loss of legal protection under CUTSA.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act,' indicating that California adheres to UTSA. The reduced timeframe undermines ongoing efforts to maintain the secrecy of valuable information, and the original agreement provided a five-year period, which likely demonstrated an acknowledgment of the lifespan of the trade secrets and the reasonable measures required to keep that information confidential.
